content:
Let \(m=n^n+1\).  Every even perfect number has the form
\[
m \;=\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1),\quad 2^p-1\text{ prime},
\]
and the known small perfect numbers are
\[
6,\;28,\;496,\;8128,\;33550336,\dots
\]
We test small \(n\):

\(n=1:\;1^1+1=2\) (not perfect)  
\(n=2:\;2^2+1=5\) (not perfect)  
\(n=3:\;3^3+1=27+1=28\), and \(28\) is perfect.  

For \(n\ge4\), one shows easily that
\[
n^n+1\;>\;2^{n-1}(2^n-1)
\quad\text{and}\quad
n^n+1\;<\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\quad\text{for any }p>n,
\]
so no further solutions occur.  Hence the only solution is
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]

Let \(m=n^n+1\).  Every even perfect number has the form
\[
m \;=\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1),\quad 2^p-1\text{ prime},
\]
and the known small perfect numbers are
\[
6,\;28,\;496,\;8128,\;33550336,\dots
\]
We test small \(n\):

\(n=1:\;1^1+1=2\) (not perfect)  
\(n=2:\;2^2+1=5\) (not perfect)  
\(n=3:\;3^3+1=27+1=28\), and \(28\) is perfect.  

For \(n\ge4\), one shows easily that
\[
n^n+1\;>\;2^{n-1}(2^n-1)
\quad\text{and}\quad
n^n+1\;<\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\quad\text{for any }p>n,
\]
so no further solutions occur.  Hence the only solution is
